When Learning, Don't Pre-Set Goals or Futures

The title of this post is going to throw people off. What does "don't pre-set goals or futures" mean? Am I saying don't think about goals or the future when learning? Let me explain why I'm framing it this way.

A lot of people who are grinding to switch careers / change tracks set themselves a goal and a future. E.g., "In X months I'm going to switch into XXXXXX..." or "X months from now I'll become XXX... and earn XXX...".
That's absolutely a good thing — it means you're giving yourself hope, a goal, an expectation. But you're going to ask: "Wait, didn't you just say don't pre-set goals or futures?"
Right. The reason I phrased it this way is that a lot of people switching careers / tracks are coming from unrelated backgrounds / unrelated fields, and they're going to hit walls hard along the way.
For example: imagine a lady who doesn't know how to cook, but dreams of becoming a chef.

Someone with no cooking — not even cutting — experience now needs to learn cooking. She has to understand seasonings, vegetables, cooking times, heat levels, etc. What looks like simple cooking is actually a chain of complex steps. So you often see successful street food / restaurants where the owner spent years researching, learning step by step — and they never tell you "I switched into being a chef X months ago!".

This post isn't here to dump negative energy on you. The point is: don't load yourself with excessive pressure while learning. When you pre-set a goal + future like "In X months I'm going to successfully switch to XXXXXX..." or "X months from now I'll become XXX... and earn XXX...", there's a real chance you start

I'm not saying pre-setting goals is bad — it's good. It means you have a direction. But you shouldn't bake
, you'll notice that hitting any bottleneck becomes urgent — you'll rush to solve it, while the clock keeps draining. What happens?
You get more and more agitated, and the agitation becomes the thing tripping you up.
Why? Because you've already hit a wall, and time pressure makes you ignore details and even short-tempered.
So the better framing is: "Once I've finished learning this, I'm going to switch careers!". That's it.

That's pretty different from "In X months I'll switch successfully!", right? Setting goals this way is healthier. Your mental state stays steadier, and when you hit a wall, you're less likely to skip over details
